speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May 3, 1996, interview with Miriam Greenstein, maiden name Kominkowska, interviewer Janice Englehart. We're in Portland, Oregon, in the United States. This interview will be conducted in English. My name is Janice Englehart. And this is Miriam Greenstein. We're in Portland, Oregon, on May 3, 1996. Mrs. Greenstein, would you begin by stating your full name? Miriam Greenstein. Do you want--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name? Kominkowska.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you state the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: October 21st, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sompolno, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that near, any larger city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. However, looking at the maps, it was all western Poland before World War-- excuse me. After World War I, it became Poland. Prior to that, parts of it were Germany and parts were Russia. And I can't tell you where the borders were, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you begin by describing your childhood in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a good childhood, happy. I was an only child, had lots of cousins. My dad owned a lumber yard. My grandparents owned a lumber mill. The family also owned virgin forests. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was a tomboy, loved to play in the wood piles. And all the workmen would create all kinds of places for myself and my two cousins. And we would create homes inside these lumber yards and, oh, take out-- if there was a knot out of a lumber board for building, those were our sausages. We had tea parties. Played many games, again, at Portland-- Portland, excuse me-- Poland public schools. And it all ended when-- before my 10th birthday, before I entered the fourth grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you get to that point, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you state your father and mother's full names, and describe a little bit about their profession and their activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Zalmen Kominkowski. My mother's first name was [? Ruta, ?] in Polish. We've always referred to her as Ruth since then-- Kominkowska. As I mentioned, my dad was a lumber man. I'm not certain whether he was an architect. I know he must have-- he had to be at least a draftsman, because he designed buildings for his customers. And also, we did have papers to leave for Australia in 1939, the winter of '39. And I remember my parents talking about it, that he got in because they needed architects under the architectural quota. Unfortunately, the Jewish quota closed. We were about 10 people away from the embassy door. And they told us to come back next year. It never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a housewife. She was a beautiful lady, pitch black, straight hair, large, almond eyes, very white skin. How does one describe a quote, a lady? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Very social, well-mannered, tremendous hostess, loved to entertain-- there was quite a bit of travel, concerts. Both of my parents loved classical music. So in those days, of course, no TVs. But there was a concert, I still remember, every Saturday morning on the radio. I don't know. It was like one-hour operas, as a rule, or operatic singers. And I remember that I had to be very quiet. As a matter of fact, I always remembered the name Jan Kiepura, a very famous Polish tenor. And I thought maybe I dreamt it. And then on TV during one of the question things-- I don't know whether you remember-- there was an opera hour when Bing was still the director of the Metro, New York Met. They had a quiz. And one of the names that came up was Jan Kiepura. So I had that verified for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They danced. They entertained. We had much company, ladies for tea in the afternoon. Of course, my father was working-- evenings, card games. My dad was a master chess player. He was also an artist. He was teaching me how to draw and paint-- good life, living out in the country. We were always in small towns, maybe because of that's where lumber yards were, or whether it was just good business, I can't tell you. I had lots of friends. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that sums it up, pretty much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you socializing with Jewish and non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. I went-- Portland public schools were church-controlled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, uh huh-- I keep saying Portland instead of Poland. [LAUGHS] Actually, the whole country was a church-controlled state. So our schools were run by the nuns even though we had teachers, public teachers. Religion was part of the education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Catholic and Jewish, believe it or not-- the Catholic youngsters had one hour twice a week. The Jewish children had one hour once a week, so that the Jewish kids had one the vacant hour. And we were excused. With my parents' approval and permission, I used to attend the Catholic religious classes-- Never hurt me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like to sit in on those classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just seemed very natural. I, I hate to admit it, I probably knew my Hail Marys better than the Jewish prayers. I did not feel different. I knew I was Jewish. I mean, we, we never denied it. I wasn't ashamed of it. But predominantly, I was Polish. There just was not a distinction in my mind. Would be no different than kids in public school here. You know, they don't say they're Catholic. They're, they're just Americans. And that's, that's pretty much the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your family's religious tradition?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends which side. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was raised probably liberal. My mother's parents-- Grandpa was very reformed, very liberal. Grandma was much more of a traditionalist. My father's parents on the other side were Hasidic Jews, very orthodox. But I grew up much closer to my maternal side of the family than my dad's. So they had a greater influence on me. I remember holidays. They were very important-- you know, the family get-togethers, the Seder, on Purim, having all the baked goodies, a Hanukkah gelt on Hanukkah. On the other hand, my grandma had an oleander tree. And she let me decorate it for Christmas. So [LAUGHS] I got my little Christmas tree. She wouldn't permit me to bring in a real one. So we were, we were definitely Jewish. But religion, at least at age nine, was not an important thing in my life. I don't know what it would have been like had I been older and had different memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spend time with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Various ways, depending on the season-- summer, no school, outdoor play, always in the lumber yard, or playing prison ball, all the physical activities, running in the fields, going out for horse and buggy rides, in the winter, playing indoor games, also outdoors on sleds all winter long, after school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Played with my dolls, and tea sets, and make believe, lots of painting, lots of artwork.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chinese checkers were very popular. So were dominoes. What else? It's been a lifetime ago-- checkers, gin rummy, [LAUGHS] and dancing. I learned how to dance when I was about seven, standing on either my father's or one of the cousins' feet. And they would lead me as we did the waltz. And the gramophone was going. As a child at that time, when you were dancing and playing games, what were your hopes for the future or dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always said I was going to work with flowers, always loved flowers. And looking back on it, I must have. Because as a mother, after my children had grown, I went back to school and received a floral design degree, along with interior design, space design. But I always knew that it had to be something to do with beauty. The other side of the coin was I'm going to be a doctor like a few of my mother's cousins. But of course, females did not become doctors then. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was a good student. I loved school. And I really, I did enjoy learning. But art was my very, just very favorite subject, always, always, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it that you first experienced anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really did not experience one-on-one anti-Semitism, which sounds very strange. But it was a class action against the Jews. It happened the day Germany entered our town. All of a sudden, I found out I was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you on that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was an alarm set. We all went to the trenches to hide, because they started to bomb [? Lebranitz. ?] And by then, the German armed forces had already entered [? Lebranitz. ?] So I understand they quickly spread the white crosses or whatever on the streets. And the bombing stopped. And we were-- we just stood there. And we watched the conquering heroes march into our little town. That's just-- I had no idea what to expect, though I knew there was a lot of anti-Semitism and discrimination. My parents, prior to the war, when we lived in Aleksandrow, used to bring many people home, usually in the evening or at night. I'd be sent to bed. And I understood that they were German Jews who were traveling through on the way to I don't know where. And my parents were among, I'm sure, some other Jewish members of the city who would go to the railroad station, bring them home, feed them, whatever else. And what I remember most is the whispering all night long. And of course, the conversation was always in German. And I just-- I had a smattering of it at that time. I learned German much later through necessity. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't understand what was. But yes, I knew it. Oh yes-- once when I was, I think, in the first or second grade, I was called a dirty Jew by a big bully in the eighth grade. What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my one and only time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, a Polish Catholic girlfriend took care of it. She was afraid I'd get beaten up, so she was more aware of it than I was. And she told him my name. She told him my name was Maria Kominkowska, and I wasn't Jewish. And I think I was too scared to argue or say anything else. And he just let me go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So yes, there must have been a lot of it. I was just fortunate. I don't know. How long did you remain in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through grade three-- I was to start the fourth grade in September of 1939. We were occupied September 1st, whatever, 10th. And the first thing that occurred is Jewish children were not permitted to attend school. This was an immediate given. They always plastered posters all over walls over town. And that was the first thing that occurred. So my formal education ended with the third grade. The other side effect of that was that you not only were not permitted to attend school, you were not allowed to have private tutoring. The consequences were dire. It could mean death for the whole family and the teacher, at least a severe beating. I did have a private tutor for probably eight months, six months, I don't know. And he used to come to our house under the guise of playing bridge with-- excuse me-- chess with my father. And they had a chess table set up by a window so they could see if anybody was coming. I was in the kitchen with art supplies-- butcher paper, paints, pencils. And underneath that would be one book, hidden. And the teacher would come and give me an assignment or check what I was doing, saunter back to their other room, make a chess move. They'd look out, see everything was clear, and come back for maybe 10 minutes. And my dad sat at the chess table the whole time. I couldn't even tell my best friends that I had a tutor. If one of my friends was seen coming, immediately, everything was hidden. It was too dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that that stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: He, along with my father, were sent to a labor camp in Germany in 1941, spring of '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that because of doing the tutoring?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All the able-bodied Jewish men, I believe age 18 to-- I don't know whether it was 40 or 50. I can't tell you-- were told to report within, I think there was maybe a 15-minute notice out in the fields outside the village. And that was it. Immediately, we followed them. But we were not permitted to go near them. Stood quite a few hundred feet away. It was such a beautiful day. I still remember the bright green meadow, the flowers, and all these men standing there, being forced to jump aboard German army trucks at gunpoint.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't take them long to load everybody up. And then the convoy took off. That's the last time I saw my dad. I wasn't 11 yet-- or was I 11? God, time flies. Spring 1941-- so no-- yeah, I was 11. And-- What did he say to you before you left, before he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't. There was no time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One minute, everything was all right. And the next minute, they were gone. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't even know what to say about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the soldiers come to your door?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't believe so. I think there was just an announcement. I don't know whether I was even home or whether I was playing. It's so vague. I've created a lot of selective amnesia through the years. And there are things I just can't pull out. They come back, but not, not every instant. It was so traumatic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my mother and her parents. We walked to the field as we watched them leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you saw the soldiers getting on the trucks, could you see your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw my father, yes, get on the truck. Soldiers stayed on the ground. They never left, just the drivers-- maybe one or two.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, I do speak at schools, and Anne Frank exhibits, and throughout Oregon. I've been asked this question so many times-- why didn't the Jews fight? Why didn't your father hide? How come all these men just went and gave themselves up?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is such a sad, simple answer to it. Nobody ever thinks of it. They had a list of every Jew in every village in every nook and cranny of Poland. If the man opted to hide, they came and killed his family. What a chance to take. They didn't dare. That's why they all went. They weren't being led to slaughter for nothing. They were doing it-- first of all, we didn't know about extermination camps. We only knew there were labor camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Secondly, they had to. If they didn't, my father would have seen my mother, and I, and her parents executed on the spot if they caught us. So what choice, unless you ran away? From then on, it was downhill. It just-- things moved so quickly. Did you still have contact with your non-Jewish friends at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- played with one girl constantly. OK, now here is a case of anti-Semitism in a way. I was enthralled by her, because they had moved to [? Lebranitz ?] from Torun, which was a big city in Poland. And I didn't know anybody who wasn't rich and very sophisticated in Torun. My mother's aunt and uncle lived there, and her cousin. And it was just a tremendous social life with magnificent homes. But the homes were apartments-- seven, eight, 10-room places. And so when I met this girl, when I heard she was from Torun, I really felt at a loss in [? Lebranitz. ?] Because I was more of a city kid than all of these kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- they were-- I don't know. At first, they were very uncomfortable with me. One girl, the first time she met me, did a deep curtsy, and tried to kiss my hand, and called me your highness. [LAUGHS] I didn't know what was going on. Can you imagine? So that this girl, I thought, well, here is somebody that I have something in common with. And every time we played together, her mother would come out and call her in. And then I found out that they had told her she couldn't play with me because I was a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not understand it. We played anyhow. And eventually, I did meet her mother. And the mother accepted me. And her dad was a caretaker to a big estate. They moved out of town. And he used to come in in the winter with a sled and horses and pick me up and take me out to play with his daughter. So maybe getting to know one Jewish kid changed their mind. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a nice life. After that, I had one other girlfriend. She was a Polish-- a German Pole. However, when Germany occupied the town, they were elevated to a high social standing by the SS. The father was not comfortable or happy with it He was a business man. And our parents were pretty good friends. And I played with their daughter. They lived a few houses away from us. As a matter of fact, this German set a radio by his window in the garden. And at night, he would turn on the news. And my father would go and hide the shadows, get the news, and then tell it to the other people, translate it into Polish. And she and I played. That came to a stop when an SS came to their home and saw me playing with her. No problem, he patted me on the head and left, of course, assuming that I was another nice German kid or a Polish Catholic maybe, even though that was frowned upon. And a week or two later, I was walking down the street with a dead chicken in my basket. I didn't bother telling you, Jews were not allowed poultry, or eggs, or much meat. We had very limited rations.
